| * [v](/wiki/Template:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Template:Transliteration of Chinese") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Template talk:Transliteration of Chinese") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Special:EditPage/Template:Transliteration of Chinese") |

The spelling of [Gwoyeu Romatzyh](/wiki/Gwoyeu_Romatzyh "Gwoyeu Romatzyh") (GR) can be divided into its treatment of [initials](/wiki/Syllable_onset "Syllable onset"), [finals](/wiki/Syllable_rime "Syllable rime") and [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"). GR uses contrasting [unvoiced/voiced](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)") pairs of consonants to represent [aspirated and unaspirated](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") initials in Chinese: for example *b* and *p* represent [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") [p] and [pʰ]. The letters *j, ch* and *sh* represent two different series of initials: the [alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant") and the [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") sounds. Although these spellings create no ambiguity in practice, readers more familiar with [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") should pay particular attention to them: GR *ju*, for example, corresponds to Pinyin *zhu*, not *ju* (which is spelled *jiu* in GR).

Many of the finals in GR are similar to those used in other [romanizations](/wiki/Chinese_romanization "Chinese romanization"). Distinctive features of GR include the use of *iu* for the [close front rounded vowel](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel") spelled *ü* or simply *u* in Pinyin. Final *-y* represents certain [allophones](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of *i*: GR *shy* and *sy* correspond to Pinyin *shi* and *si* respectively.

The most striking feature of GR is its treatment of tones. The first tone is represented by the basic form of each syllable, the spelling being modified according to precise but complex [rules](#Tonal_rules) for the other three tones. For example the syllable spelled *ai* (first tone) becomes *ai**r**, a**e*** and *a**y*** in the other tones. A neutral (unstressed) tone can optionally be indicated by preceding it with a dot or full stop: for example *perng.yeou* "friend".

[Rhotacization](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua"), a common feature of [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin "Standard Mandarin") (especially [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect")) Chinese, is marked in GR by the suffix *-l*. Owing to the rather complex orthographical details, a given rhotacized form may correspond to more than one non-rhotacized syllable: for example, *jiel* can mean either "today" (from *jin*) or "chick" (from *ji*).

A number of frequently occurring [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") have abbreviated spellings in GR. The most common of these, followed by their Pinyin equivalents, are: *-g* (-ge), *-j* (-zhe), *-m* (-me), *sh* (shi) and *-tz* (-zi).

## Basic forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Basic forms")]

GR, like [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"), uses contrasting [unvoiced/voiced](/wiki/Voice_(phonetics) "Voice (phonetics)") pairs of consonants to represent [aspirated and unaspirated](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") sounds in Chinese. For example *b* and *p* represent [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") [p] and [pʰ] (*p* and  *p'*  in [Wade-Giles](/wiki/Wade-Giles "Wade-Giles")). Another feature of GR surviving in Pinyin is the representation of words (usually of two syllables) as units: e.g. *Beeijing* rather than the Wade-Giles *Pei3-ching1*.

The basic features of GR spelling are shown in the following tables of initials and finals, the latter referring to the basic T1 forms.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Many of the spelling features are the same as in Pinyin; differences are highlighted in the tables and [discussed in detail](#Spelling) after the second table. The [rules of tonal spelling](#Tonal_rules) follow in a separate section.

In the tables Pinyin spellings are given only where they differ from GR, in which case they appear in (parentheses). The tables also give the [pronunciation](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") in [brackets].

### Initials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Initials")]

|  | | [Labial](/wiki/Labial_consonant "Labial consonant") | [Alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") | [Retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") | [Alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant") | [Velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Nasal](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") | | [**m** [m]](/wiki/Bilabial_nasal "Bilabial nasal") ㄇ | [**n** [n]](/wiki/Alveolar_nasal "Alveolar nasal") ㄋ |  |  |  |
| [Plosive](/wiki/Plosive_consonant "Plosive consonant") | [unaspirated](/wiki/Tenuis_consonant "Tenuis consonant") | [**b** [p]](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive") ㄅ | [**d** [t]](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive") ㄉ |  |  | [**g** [k]](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_plosive "Voiceless velar plosive") ㄍ |

### Spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spelling")]

Further information: [Comparison of Chinese Phonetic Systems](/wiki/Comparison_of_Chinese_Phonetic_Systems "Comparison of Chinese Phonetic Systems")

GR basic (T1) spellings are compared to the spelling conventions of Pinyin in the [table](#Pinyin_comparison:_basic_forms) below. A [separate table](#Pinyin_comparison:_all_tones), after the [tonal rules](#Tonal_rules), compares spellings using all four tones.

#### Alveolar and retroflex series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Alveolar and retroflex series")]

The letter *j* and the [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") *ch* and *sh* represent two different series of sounds. When followed by *i* they correspond to the [alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant") sounds (Pinyin *j*, *q*, and *x*); otherwise they correspond to the [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") sounds (Pinyin *zh*, *ch*, and *sh*). In practice this feature creates no ambiguity, because the two series of consonants are in [complementary distribution](/wiki/Complementary_distribution "Complementary distribution"). Nevertheless it does make the correspondence between GR and Pinyin spellings difficult to follow. In some cases they agree (*chu* is the same syllable in both systems); but in other cases they differ—sometimes confusingly so (for example, GR *ju*, *jiu* and *jiou* correspond to Pinyin *zhu*, *ju* and *jiu* respectively).

This potential for confusion can be seen graphically in the [table of initials](#Initials), where the bold letters **j, ch** and **sh** cut across the highlighted division between alveolo-palatal and retroflex.

#### Other differences from Pinyin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other differences from Pinyin")]

GR also differs from Pinyin in its transcription of vowels and semivowels:

* GR uses *iu* for the [close front rounded vowel](/wiki/Close_front_rounded_vowel "Close front rounded vowel") (IPA y) spelled *ü* or in many cases simply *u* in Pinyin. (The [contracted Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin#Letters "Pinyin") *iu* is written *iou* in GR.)
* Final *-y* represents the [ɨ] [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of *i*: GR *shy* and *sy* correspond to Pinyin *shi* and *si* respectively.
* No basic forms in GR begin with *w-* or *y-*: Pinyin *ying* and *wu* are written *ing* and *u* in GR ([but only in T1](#uiNote)).

Other important GR spellings which differ from Pinyin include:

* GR writes *au* for Pinyin *ao* (but see the [rule for T3](#T3Rule)).
* *el* corresponds to Pinyin *er* (*-r* being reserved to [indicate T2](#T2Rule)). The most important use of *-(e)l* is as a [rhotacization](#Rhotacization) suffix.
* GR uses *ts* for Pinyin *c* and *tz* for Pinyin *z*.
* *-uen* and *-uei* correspond to the contracted Pinyin forms *-un* and *-ui*.
* GR also has three letters for dialectal sounds: *v* (万 in extended [Zhuyin](/wiki/Zhuyin "Zhuyin")), *ng* (兀), and *gn* (广).

As in Pinyin, an [apostrophe](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe") is used to clarify syllable divisions. *Pin'in*, the GR spelling of the word "Pinyin", is itself a good example: the apostrophe shows that the compound is made up of *pin* + *in* rather than *pi* + *nin*.

### Pinyin comparison: basic forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pinyin comparison: basic forms")]

The following list summarizes the differences between GR and Pinyin spelling. The list is in GR alphabetical order (click the button next to the heading to change to Pinyin order).

| GR | Pinyin |
| --- | --- |
| au | ao |
| ch(i) | q |
| è | ê |
| el | er |
| iau | iao |
| iou | iu |
| iu | u (qu), ü |
| iue | ue (que), üe |
| iuan | uan (quan) |
| iun | un (qun) |
| j(a,e,u,y) | zh |
| sh(i) | x |
| ts | c |
| tz | z |
| uei | ui |
| uen | un (chun) |
| y (final) | i (zhi, ci, shi) |

## Tonal rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tonal rules")]

:   *Note:* In this section the word "[tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)")" is abbreviated as "T": thus T1 stands for Tone 1, or first tone, T2 stands for Tone 2, or second tone, etc.

Wherever possible GR indicates tones 2, 3 and 4 by respelling the [basic T1 form](#Basic_forms) of the syllable, replacing a vowel with another having a similar sound (*i* with *y* or *e*, for example). But this concise procedure cannot be applied in every case, since the syllable may not contain a suitable vowel for modification. In such cases a letter (*r* or *h*) is added or inserted instead. The precise rule to be followed in any specific case is determined by the rules given below.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

A colour-coded **rule of thumb** is given below for each tone: the same colours are used below in a [list of provinces](#Pinyin_comparison:_all_tones). Each rule of thumb is then amplified by a comprehensive set of rules for that tone. These codes are used in the rules:

:   * V = a vowel
    * NV = a non-vowel (either a consonant or zero in the case of an initial vowel)
    * ⇏ = "but avoid forming [the specified combination]"

Pinyin equivalents are given in brackets after each set of examples. To illustrate the GR tonal rules in practice, a [table](#Pinyin_comparison:_all_tones) comparing Pinyin and GR spellings of some Chinese provinces follows the detailed rules.

**Tone 1 (high): basic form**

* Initial [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant") (*l-/m-/n-/r-*): insert *-h-* as second letter. *r**h**eng, m**h**a* (rēng, mā)
* Otherwise use the [basic form](#Description).

**Tone 2 (rising): i/u → y/w; or add -r**

* Initial sonorants: use basic form. *reng, ma* (réng, má)
* N*i*V → N*y*V ( + *-i* if final). *chyng, chyan, yng, yan, pyi* (qíng, qián, yíng, yán, pí)
* N*u*V → N*w*V ( + *-u* if final). *chwan, wang, hwo, chwu* (chuán, wáng, huó, chú)
* Otherwise add *r* to vowel or [diphthong](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"). *charng, bair* (cháng, bái)

**Tone 3 (dipping/falling): i/u → e/o; or double vowel**

* V*i* or *i*V → V*e* or *e*V (⇏*ee*). *chean, bae, sheau* (qiǎn, bǎi, xiǎo), but not ~~gee~~
* V*u* or *u*V → V*o* or *o*V (⇏*oo*). *doan, dao, shoei* (duǎn, dǎo, shuǐ), but not ~~hoo~~
* When both *i* and *u* can be found, only the first one changes, i.e. *jeau, goai, sheu* (jiǎo, guǎi, xǔ), not ~~jeao~~, ~~goae~~, ~~sheo~~
* For basic forms starting with *i-/u-*, change the starting *i-/u-* to *e-/o-* and add initial *y-/w-*. *yean, woo, yeu* (yǎn, wǒ, yǔ)
* Otherwise double the (main)[[3]](#cite_note-3) vowel. *chiing, daa, geei, huoo, goou* (qǐng, dǎ, gěi, huǒ, gǒu)

**Tone 4 (falling): change/double final letter; or add -h**

* V*i* → V*y*. *day, suey* (dài, suì)
* V*u* → V*w* (⇏*iw*). *daw, gow* (dào, gòu), but not ~~chiw~~
* *-n* → *-nn*. *duann* (duàn)
* *-l* → *-ll*. *ell* (èr)
* *-ng* → *-nq*. *binq* (bìng)
* Otherwise add *h*. *dah, chiuh, dih* (dà, qù, dì)
* For basic forms starting with *i-/u-*, replace initial *i-/u-* with *y-/w-*, in addition to the necessary tonal change. *yaw, wuh* (yào, wù)

**Neutral tone (轻声 *Chingsheng / qīngshēng*)**

A dot (usually written as a period or full stop) may be placed before neutral tone (unstressed) syllables, which appear in their original tonal spelling: *perng.yeou, dih.fang* (péngyou, dìfang). [Y.R. Chao](/wiki/Yuen_Ren_Chao "Yuen Ren Chao") used this device in the first eight chapters of the *Mandarin Primer*, restricting it thereafter to new words on their first appearance. In *A Grammar of Spoken Chinese* he introduced a subscript circle (˳) to indicate an optional neutral tone, as in *bujy˳daw*, "don't know" (Pinyin pronunciation *bùzhīdào* or *bùzhīdao*).

**GR *u-* and *i-* syllables**

Any GR syllables beginning *u-* or *i-* must be T1: in T2, T3 and T4 these syllables all begin with *w-* or *y-* respectively. An example in all four tones is the following: *ing, yng, yiing, yinq* (Pinyin *ying*).

## Rhotacization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rhotacization")]

*[Erhua](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua")* ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 儿化; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 兒化), or the [rhotacized](/wiki/R-colored_vowel "R-colored vowel") or retroflex[[5]](#cite_note-5) ending, is indicated in GR by *-l* rather than *-r*, which is already used as a [T2 marker](#T2Rule). The appropriate [tonal modification](#Tonal_rules) is then applied to the basic rhotacized form: for example *shell* (Pinyin *shìr*) from the basic form *shel*, and *deal* (*diǎnr*) from the basic form *dial*.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the fourth tone, certain syllables don't double the *l* but are instead spelled by first writing the non-rhotacized syllable in the fourth tone and then adding *l*: *(-i/y)awl, (-i/y)owl, (-i/y/-u/w/)anql, (-i/y/w)enql, (-i/y)onql, ehl* (from *e’l*, the basic rhotacized form of *e*; compare *ell* from *el*, which is both the basic rhotacized form of *en, ei,* and *y* and a basic Mandarin syllable).

Most other romanization systems preserve the [underlying form](/wiki/Underlying_form "Underlying form"), but GR transcribes the surface form as pronounced. These are the principles followed to create the basic form of a rhotacized syllable in GR:[[7]](#cite_note-7)

1. *-l* is added to the final's basic non-rhotacized form
2. *-y* becomes *-e-*
3. *i* becomes *ie-*, and *iu* becomes *iue-*
4. *in* becomes *ie-*, and *iun* becomes *iue-*; in all other cases, *-n* disappears without trace
5. *ing* becomes *ieng-*
6. final asyllabic *-i* (found in *(i/u)ai* and *(u)ei*) disappears
7. with the final *e*, an apostrophe is added before the *-l*, i.e. *e’l, er’l, ee’l* (to separate them from *el, erl, eel*), except in the fourth tone, where the spelling is *ehl* (as this is sufficient to separate it from *ell*)
8. with the finals *ie* and *iue*, an apostrophe is added in the first and second tones only, i.e. *ie’l, ye’l, -ieel/yeel, -iell/yell* and *iue’l, yue’l, -yeuel/-euel, -iuell/yuell*

Thus, the basic rhotacized final

* **el** corresponds to the basic non-rhotacized finals *en, ei,* and *-y* and is also a basic Mandarin syllable
* **uel** corresponds to *uen* and *uei*
* **iel** corresponds to *i* and *in*; in the third and fourth tones, it also corresponds to *ie*
* **iuel** corresponds to *iu* and *iun*; in the third and fourth tones, it also corresponds to *iue*
* **al** corresponds to *a, an,* and *ai*
* **ial** corresponds to *ia, ian,* and *iai*
* **ual** corresponds to *ua, uan,* and *uai*

As a consequence, the [one-to-one correspondence](/wiki/One-to-one_correspondence "One-to-one correspondence") between GR and Pinyin is broken, since one GR rhotacized form may correspond to several Pinyin forms. For example, *jiel* corresponds to both *jīr* and *jīnr* (both pronounced [t͡ɕjɚ˥]), and *jial* corresponds to both *jiār* and *jiānr* (both pronounced [t͡ɕjaɚ̯˥]).

## Tone sandhi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Tone sandhi")]

The most important manifestation of [tone sandhi](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology#Tone_sandhi "Standard Chinese phonology") in Mandarin is the change of a T3 syllable to T2 when followed by another T3 syllable (T3 + T3 → T2 + T3). GR does not reflect this change in the spelling: the word for "fruit" is written *shoeiguoo*, even though the pronunciation is *shweiguoo*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Four common words with more complicated tone sandhi (also ignored in the spelling) are mentioned below under [Exceptions](#Exceptions).

## Abbreviations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Abbreviations")]

A number of frequently-occurring [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") have abbreviated spellings in GR.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The commonest of these, followed by their Pinyin equivalents, are:

* *-g* (*-ge*)
* *-j* (*-zhe*)
* *-m* (*-me*)

:   occurs in *sherm* (*shénme*), *jemm/tzemm* (*zhème*) and *tzeem* (*zěnme*)

* *sh* (*shi*)

:   also in compounds such as *jiowsh* (*jiùshi*), *dannsh* (*dànshi*), etc.

* *-tz* (*-zi*)

## Reduplication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Reduplication")]

In its original form GR used the two "spare" letters of the alphabet, *v* and *x*, to indicate [reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication"). This mimicked the method by which the Japanese writing system indicates repeated [Kanji characters](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") with an [iteration mark](/wiki/Iteration_mark "Iteration mark") (々). In GR the letter *x* indicates that the preceding syllable is repeated (*shieh.x = shieh.shieh*, "thank you"), *vx* being used when the preceding *two* syllables are repeated (*haoshuo vx! = haoshuo haoshuo!* "you're too kind!").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

This concise but completely unphonetic, and hence unintuitive, device appears in Chao's *Mandarin Primer* and all W. Simon's texts (including his *Chinese-English Dictionary*). Eventually, however, it was silently discarded even by its inventor: in Chao's *Grammar* as well as his *Sayable Chinese* all reduplicated syllables are written out in full in their GR transcription.

## Exceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spelling_in_Gwoyeu_Romatzyh&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Exceptions")]

The following words and [characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") do not follow the rules of GR:

* The name *Romatzyh* (which strictly speaking should be "Luomaatzyh") follows international usage ([Roma](/wiki/Rome "Rome")).
* The characters 一 ("one"), 七 ("seven"), 八 ("eight"), and 不 ("no/not") are always written *i*, *chi*, *ba*, and *bu*, respectively, regardless of the tone in which they are pronounced. In other words changes due to [tone sandhi](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology#Tones_on_special_syllables "Standard Chinese phonology") are not reflected in GR.

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** See [Chao (1948)](#CITEREFChao1948), pp. 19–24 and [Chao (1968a)](#CITEREFChao1968a), pp. 20–25 for tables and fuller discussion.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** The rules are given, though in a different form, in [Chao (1948)](#CITEREFChao1948), pp. 28–30 (synopsis p. 336) and [Chao (1968a)](#CITEREFChao1968a), pp. 29–30 (synopsis p. 847). See also Table IX in [Simon (1947)](#CITEREFSimon1947), p. lviii.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** In diphthongs the main vowel is the vowel bearing the tone mark in Pinyin.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** For a complete list of provinces in GR, characters and "map spelling", see [Simon (1947)](#CITEREFSimon1947): Table XV(1),c.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** This is Chao's terminology: see [Chao (1968a)](#CITEREFChao1968a), p. 46.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** The temptation to read these examples as the English words *shell* and *deal* must be resisted.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** For a detailed discussion of the spelling of these endings in GR, see [Chao (1968a)](#CITEREFChao1968a), p. 46-52 and Table IX in [Simon (1947)](#CITEREFSimon1947), p. lix.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** In the first eight chapters of [Chao (1948)](#CITEREFChao1948) such syllables are printed in italics as a reminder to students (e.g. "*shoei*guoo").
9. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** These and other abbreviations are listed in [Chao (1968a)](#CITEREFChao1968a), p. xxx.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** These symbols are introduced unobtrusively in endnotes to Lessons 2 and 4 on pp 131 and 146 of [Chao (1948)](#CITEREFChao1948)—the explanation of *vx* being further hidden in a parenthesis.
